title: I Submitted My Models to Intel's Low-Bit LLM Leaderboard—Here's How They Stack Up
summary: I submitted seven of my models to Intel's Low-Bit Open LLM Leaderboard to see how they perform when quantized. The results: a 4B distilled Claude model landing in the top 30% overall, 1.2B LFM2.5 models trading blows with popular 1B baselines, and tiny 0.2B experiments that end up in the same performance band as OpenAI's 20B GPT-oss-20B.
content:
Sending My Models to Intel's Low-Bit Leaderboard

Instead of just eyeballing local tests, I wanted a clean apples-to-apples comparison of my models against a wide range of open LLMs in low-bit form. So I submitted seven of them to the Intel Low-Bit Open LLM Leaderboard and waited for the runs to finish.

The leaderboard currently has 71 entries across a range of sizes—from tiny 150M models up to 30B+ monsters—so it's a decent stress test of where my stuff actually lands.

The figure above is a zoom-in around my submissions—orange dots and bars are my models, grey are nearby baselines. But that's just the neighborhood view. Let's look at the global rankings.

Where My Models Land on the Full Leaderboard

Out of 71 evaluated entries, here's a snapshot of my seven submissions:

Global RankModelAverageHellaSwagMMLUPiQASize (GB)
21FlameF0X/Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (NVFP4-RTN)62.6848.4666.4773.123.89
22FlameF0X/Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (MXFP4-RTN)61.1846.7164.9671.873.81
45FlameF0X/LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (W4A16-RTN)46.7639.5131.9968.770.75
47FlameF0X/LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX (W4A16-RTN)45.2539.7026.5669.480.76
63FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-pt (W4A16-RTN)34.4025.5923.2154.410.30
65FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-it (W4A16-RTN)34.2025.7423.7753.100.30
66FlameF0X/LFM2-Research (W4A16-Tuning)34.0326.3324.1851.580.06

One model in the top 30%, two in the mid-pack, and three tiny experiments down in the 30–35% band—though notably sharing that band with a 20B model from OpenAI.

The 4B Model: Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6

The strongest performer is Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6, a 4B parameter multimodal model distilled from Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking (the claude.ai version). The goal was to see how much of Claude's reasoning style and conversational behavior I could compress into something that still runs on decent consumer hardware.

On the low-bit leaderboard:

  • NVFP4-RTN lands at rank 21/71 with a 62.68% average score.
  • MXFP4-RTN lands at rank 22/71 with a 61.18% average score.

Both sit right behind Qwen's own Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 and ahead of the base Qwen3-4B MXFP4 quantization—not bad for a distilled variant.

Under the hood it's a chat-optimized, multimodal Qwen3-4B with full Transformers + vLLM support, plus Docker and SGLang recipes on the card. If you want to plug it in and poke at it:

  • FlameF0X/Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6

1.2B Liquid AI Experiments: Liquid Claude & Liquid CodeX

Next are my two 1.2B LFM2.5 experiments. Both use Liquid AI's non-transformer LFM2.5 architecture, which already has a lean toward reasoning. I used it as a small, fast canvas and asked: what happens if you apply Claude-style thinking or heavy code training on top?

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 — "Liquid Claude"
    A straight distillation of Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking into LiquidAI/LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking via SFT. On Intel's metrics, it hits:
    • Average: 46.76%
    • HellaSwag: 39.51
    • MMLU: 31.99
    • PiQA: 68.77
    That lands it at rank 45/71, trading blows with other ~1B-ish baselines like MiniCPM5-1B and the smaller Qwen3.5/2.5 variants.
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX — "Liquid CodeX"
    Same backbone, different training. Fine-tuned on 2M code examples with explicit chain-of-thought from Modotte/CodeX-2M-Thinking. The result is a coding-focused model with:
    • Average: 45.25%
    • HellaSwag: 39.70
    • MMLU: 26.56
    • PiQA: 69.48
    That puts it at rank 47/71, slightly behind Liquid Claude but still in that mid-pack band where the decent 1B-ish models live.

Both are Apache 2.0, work with standard Transformers and vLLM, and have low-bit variants targeted at typical local setups (llama.cpp, LM Studio, Jan/Ollama, etc.). Links:

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 (Liquid Claude)
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX (Liquid CodeX)

Stupidly Small Models Doing Surprisingly Okay

Then there are the toys: two 0.2B Qwen2 models and a tiny LFM2-Research model trained only on arXiv AI/ML text. On paper they shouldn't matter much—but their low-bit scores put them in the same neighborhood as OpenAI's gpt-oss-20b quantizations.

Qwen2-0.2B-pt & Qwen2-0.2B-it

  • Qwen2-0.2B-pt — a 200M parameter pretrain on a mix of Salesforce/wikitext, roneneldan/TinyStories, and Skylion007/openwebtext. No instructions, just a straight LM. It lands at rank 63/71 with 34.40% average.
  • Qwen2-0.2B-it — instruction-tuned from the pt model using a mix of chat and reasoning datasets (HuggingFaceH4/ultrachat_200k, openai/gsm8k, microsoft/orca-math-word-problems-200k, laion/OIG, microsoft/wiki_qa, etc.). It lands at rank 65/71 with 34.20% average.

These numbers are obviously not leaderboard-topping, but here's the interesting part: OpenAI/gpt-oss-20b (20 billion parameters) shows up with three different low-bit entries in essentially the same band—ranks 60, 64, and 67 with ~34–36% averages. So a 0.2B side-project model ends up hanging out with a 20B model once everything is crushed down to 4-bit.

Links if you want to mess with them:

  • FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-pt
  • FlameF0X/Qwen2-0.2B-it

LFM2-Research: Tiny ArXiv Brain

LFM2-Research isn't meant to be a general-purpose chat model at all—it's a small LFM2 causal LM trained purely on AI/ML arXiv papers (FlameF0X/arXiv-AI-ML).

Architecture-wise it's modest: 8 layers, hidden size 512, 8 heads with grouped query attention (4 KV heads), 2K context, and trained on 2,500 samples for 20 epochs. Very narrow, probably overfit, and completely unaligned.

On the Intel low-bit board, it still clocks a 34.03% average at rank 66/71, right in the same low-30s band as GPT-oss-20b's quantized runs. Not bad for a 60MB domain-specific research toy.

You can grab it here:

  • FlameF0X/LFM2-Research

Why This Was Worth Doing

Putting all of these on the same low-bit leaderboard made a few things clear:

  • Distillation is genuinely competitive. The Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 models sit right behind Qwen's own 4B Thinking model, which is a reasonable result for a distilled variant at this size.
  • Tiny models can be useful. Once you crush everything to 4-bit, a well-trained 0.2B model isn't that far off a quantized 20B in this benchmark regime. They're not interchangeable, but "small and decent" is a real design point.
  • Low-bit evals are their own thing. If you care about running on laptops, phones, or edge hardware, FP16 leaderboards don't tell the whole story. This leaderboard is explicitly about what survives quantization, which is a different game.

Where I Want to Take This Next

After seeing these numbers, a few obvious follow-ups:

  • Iterate on Qwen3-4B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 with more aggressive quantization and possibly a dedicated reasoning-only variant.
  • Clean up and slightly scale the 0.2B Qwen2 family to see how far that tiny frontier can go before it stops being worth it.
  • Do a second round of LFM2-Research with more arXiv data and a small alignment head to see how much utility you can squeeze out of a very domain-specific tiny LM.

All of these are fully open and runnable locally. If you benchmark them against your own baselines or fine-tune any of them, I'd love to see what you get.

title: FWKV: I Built My Own Language Model Architecture From Scratch
summary: FWKV (Feed-forward Weighted Key Value) is a novel recurrent LM architecture I designed and built from the ground up. It replaces attention with a single multiplicative state update — and it's already faster than Transformers at scale.
content:
What Is FWKV?

FWKV — short for Feed-forward Weighted Key Value (or Floored Weighted Key Value, depending on who's asking) — is a language model architecture I designed from scratch. It's not a Transformer. It's not a copy of RWKV. It's built around two specific ideas I wanted to explore.

The Core Idea

The whole architecture rests on two design decisions:

  • FFNN as the primary compute block — fast, parallelizable, and hardware-friendly
  • A floored multiplicative recurrent state as the sole context mechanism — no attention, no KV cache bloat

The state update equation is the heart of the model:

S_t = (S_{t-1} ⊙ W) + k_t ⊙ v_t

At each timestep, the previous state gets decayed by a learned weight matrix W, then updated with the outer product of the current key and value vectors. The crucial constraint: W is floored above zero, so the model can never fully forget. This gives stable, bounded memory without gating complexity.

The Models

The FWKV collection currently has three open models, all Apache 2.0 licensed:

ModelParametersTraining DataLink
FWKV-1-hf28.9MSalesforce/wikitextHF
FWKV-50M50.6MSalesforce/wikitextHF
FWKV-TinyStories—roneneldan/TinyStoriesHF

All models use the custom fwkv architecture and are fully compatible with Transformers and vLLM.

Speed: Why This Matters

The latency numbers are the real argument for this architecture. Benchmarked on CUDA against a standard Transformer and RWKV across model sizes from 10M to 1B parameters, FWKV sits somewhat above RWKV on latency. At 1B parameters, Transformers clock in at ~82 seconds for 1024 tokens. RWKV gets down to ~19 seconds. FWKV lands around ~39 seconds, with the error bars showing variance across runs.

That might look like FWKV is slower than RWKV at 1B parameters—and right now it is, mostly due to hardware bottlenecks rather than theoretical limits. Theoretically it should land closer to RWKV's range. But the bigger point is that FWKV's design is simpler: no complex gating, no CUDA-specific kernel dependency, and the FFNN backbone is trivially parallelizable. The architecture is meant to be a clean, hackable foundation, not a production inference engine yet.

Try It

There's a live demo Space where you can prompt the model and see it generate text directly in your browser:

  • FWKV Demo on Hugging Face Spaces
  • FWKV-50M Demo
  • Full FWKV Collection

The architecture is young but the foundations are solid. More sizes and training runs are coming.

title: From 30M Parameters to #1: My Chess Model Just Took the Top Spot
summary: I was checking the community spaces and noticed that ChessSLM, my tiny 30M parameter chess model, just climbed to the #1 spot on Maxime Labonne's leaderboard with a peak Elo of 1146. Here is how I put it together.
content:
ChessSLM Reached the Top of the Leaderboard

I was checking the community spaces and noticed that ChessSLM is sitting at rank #1 on Maxime Labonne's leaderboard with a peak Elo of 1146, ahead of several much larger models. Worth writing up how it got there.

What is ChessSLM?

ChessSLM is a specialized small language model that plays chess using autoregressive natural language move generation. Instead of evaluating board states like a traditional engine, it treats chess purely as a language modeling problem.

The model is built on a compact GPT-2 architecture (~30M parameters). I pre-trained it from scratch on 100,000 chess games sourced from the mlabonne/chessllm dataset using Standard Algebraic Notation (SAN).

The Up-to-Date Leaderboard

Here is how the top of the field currently looks on the official ChessLLM evaluation space:

RankModelElo Rating
1FlameF0X/ChessSLM1146
2DedeProGames/mini-chennus1115
3EleutherAI/pythia-70m-deduped1106
4nlpguy/smolchess-v21092
5nlpguy/amdchess-v91092
6DedeProGames/dialochess1078
7FlameF0X/ChessSLM-RL1056

Capabilities & Match Performance

ChessSLM is a decent example of what tightly focused training data can do for a tiny model. In head-to-head evaluation matches against major LLM baselines, it managed to split results against some larger models—including a win against Claude, though it dropped a match against Gemini.

Current Limitations

Because it operates strictly as a generative language model without an integrated rules engine, it has some real quirks:

  • Illegal Move Hallucinations: The model will occasionally try to play moves that violate chess rules, since it generates text based on historical patterns rather than legal validation.
  • No State Verification: There is no board state checking mechanism acting as an anchor. It just reads and writes pure notation history strings.
  • Limited Strategic Depth: While it holds up at lower Elo levels, it isn't going to match a dedicated chess engine like Stockfish.

What's Next?

The result suggests the approach has legs. Moving forward, I want to experiment with adding move legality filters, integrating board state validation, training on larger datasets, and setting up reinforcement learning cycles through self-play.

You can play against ChessSLM directly on Hugging Face.

title: Introducing LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 & LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX
summary: Two new fine-tunes of Liquid AI's LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking: one distilled from Claude Sonnet 4.6's adaptive reasoning, the other trained on 2M coding examples with chain-of-thought thinking. Compact, open, and surprisingly capable.
content:
Two New Models

I just pushed two new fine-tunes to Hugging Face. Here's what they are, why I built them, and how to run them.

LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 — "Liquid Claude"

This is a knowledge distillation of Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking — specifically the version you interact with on claude.ai, not the API variant — into Liquid AI's LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking base via SFT. The training data (sourced from FlameF0X/Claude-Sonnet-4.6) is private for now, but the model itself is fully open under Apache 2.0.

The goal was simple: squeeze some of Claude's reasoning style and chain-of-thought behavior into a 1.2B parameter model that anyone can run locally. It tags reasoning, cot, and distillate for a reason — the model tends to think before it answers, which is a direct inheritance from the teacher model.

It picked up 1.7K downloads in its first day, so clearly people were curious.

"Model trained on Claude Sonnet 4.6 Adaptive Thinking (The claude.ai Claude, not the API Claude). The training data is PRIVATE."

LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX — "Liquid CodeX"

This one takes a different angle. Instead of distilling a general-purpose model, I fine-tuned LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking on the Modotte/CodeX-2M-Thinking dataset — 2 million coding examples formatted with thinking traces. The result is a compact model that does reasonably well at code generation and technical reasoning.

Training was done with TRL's SFT trainer, and like its sibling, it's Apache 2.0 licensed and runs with standard Transformers or vLLM.

Benchmark Results

Here is how the models stack up on standard evaluations according to the Intel Low Bit Open LLM Leaderboard:

ModelAverageHellaSwagMMLUPiqaSource
LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.646.7639.5131.9968.77Intel/low bit open llm leaderboard
LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX45.2539.7026.5669.48Intel/low bit open llm leaderboard

Why LFM2.5?

Liquid AI's LFM2.5 architecture is a non-transformer sequence model that holds up well at the 1.2B scale. It's fast, memory-efficient, and the Thinking variant already has reasoning baked in—which makes it a natural canvas for distillation experiments like these.

Links

  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Distilled-Claude-4.6 on Hugging Face
  • LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking-CodeX on Hugging Face

Both are free, open, and ready to run.
